Invoke any subagent via the generic wrapper (Bash, foreground):

```
python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
  --name <name> \
  --slug <kebab-slug> \
  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
```

When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt and directive shape from `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` before composing the directive.

**Each [HIGHLY RECOMMENDED] subagent should be dispatched this phase unless you have a concrete reason to skip.** For this phase that means: `blueprint-reviewer`, `progress-critic`, `strategy-critic`. When you choose to skip one (e.g. STRATEGY.md unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND, or no new prover output to assess), record the rationale as a one-line bullet under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`:

```markdown
## Subagent skips

- <subagent-name>: <one-line reason, naming the condition that justifies the skip>
```

A post-phase audit reads that section and silences its warning for subagents you skipped with rationale; it warns only for subagents that neither dispatched nor were named under `## Subagent skips`. Filling templates with hollow dispatches when nothing has changed is the failure mode this affordance exists to avoid — be willing to skip when the input hasn't changed.

## Workflow guidance from active subagents

Each enabled subagent below carries usage instructions for you (the dispatching agent). Read these as workflow rules that apply this iteration — they encode how to USE the subagent and when in your phase to dispatch it.

### blueprint-reviewer

- Dispatch me BEFORE writing any prover objectives or touching Lean files.
  The plan agent's job is to make sure the blueprint is complete and
  detailed enough first; only then should provers be assigned. A weak
  blueprint produces low-quality prover work that the next iter then
  has to throw away.
- I am highly recommended every plan phase. The audit warning fires
  if I am skipped without a recorded rationale.

  **You may skip me this iter when ALL of:**
    - no chapter under `blueprint/src/chapters/` was edited since my
      prior dispatch (check via `git diff --stat HEAD~N
      blueprint/src/chapters/` where N spans iters back to my last
      run);
    - my prior verdict cleared the HARD GATE for all chapters
      currently under active prover work;
    - no must-fix-this-iter finding from my prior dispatch remains
      live (every flagged chapter has either been writer-patched or
      dropped from objectives).

  Record the skip under `## Subagent skips` in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`
  with the one-line rationale. Do NOT skip me when the prior verdict
  flagged any chapter `partial | false` and that chapter still feeds
  a live prover lane — the HARD GATE depends on a current audit.
- I always read the WHOLE blueprint. Do not pass me a scope-limiting
  directive — even when the iteration's focus is narrow, the cross-
  chapter view is the point of running me.
- Read my per-chapter checklist and use it to decide which chapters
  need a follow-up writer dispatch this iter (consult your catalog
  for the blueprint-writing subagent). You do not need to re-read
  the chapters yourself; the checklist is your view into them.

### HARD GATE — per-file prover dispatch

This is the rule that protects the project from low-quality prover work.
Apply it verbatim, every iter, no exceptions:

- For each `.lean` file F you are considering adding to
  `## Current Objectives` (i.e. about to send a prover to), identify
  the corresponding blueprint chapter C (the `Foo/Bar.lean →
  Foo_Bar.tex` slug mapping). Look up C in my per-chapter checklist.
- If C has `complete: true` AND `correct: true` AND no must-fix-this-iter
  finding touches it, F may go into the objectives.
- Otherwise (C is `partial | false` on either axis, OR a must-fix
  finding names C, OR a broken `\uses{}` in C points at a label F's
  blueprint depends on):
  1. DROP F from this iter's objectives. Defer the prover round on F
     to the next iter.
  2. Dispatch the catalog's blueprint-writing subagent for C THIS
     iter with a directive targeting the specific must-fix items I
     flagged.
  3. Record in iter/iter-NNN/plan.md why F was deferred (cite the
     reviewer findings).
- Re-dispatching me after the writer returns is optional within the
  same iter — the next iter's mandatory dispatch of me will confirm.

### Strategy / multi-route handling

- If the strategy has multiple viable routes and I report that one
  or more routes have no blueprint coverage, dispatch the catalog's
  blueprint-writing subagent (one call per missing-coverage route)
  in the same iteration. Do not let provers begin work on a route
  until its blueprint coverage is in place.
- If I flag a definition that may require a strategy modification,
  treat that as a STRATEGY.md update task before any further Lean work
  — provers cannot be dispatched until the strategy update is reflected
  in the blueprint.

### What "deferred" means in practice

Deferring a prover round is the correct, intentional action — not a
failure. The 1-iter latency cost of waiting for a writer is small
compared to the cost of a prover formalizing a broken blueprint and
the work being thrown away. Log the deferral cleanly in plan.md and
move on; the next iter's mandatory me-dispatch will green-light F.

### blueprint-writer

- Dispatch one writer per chapter that the most recent blueprint
  review (the blueprint-review subagent in your catalog, when
  present) flagged as incomplete, lacking proof detail, or missing
  multi-route coverage.
- Each directive must be precise: strategy context (the slice that
  matters for this chapter), required definitions/theorems (with
  enough mathematical detail to formalize), references, and
  explicit out-of-scope items. Writers do NOT speculate beyond
  what the directive lists.
- **NEVER instruct the writer to add ``\leanok`` or ``\mathlibok``
  markers** (no "after writing the block, add `\leanok`"; no
  "verification: confirm `\leanok` is present"). The writer's
  descriptor forbids it — ``\leanok`` is managed by the
  deterministic ``sync_leanok`` phase, ``\mathlibok`` by the review
  agent. A directive that asks the writer to add markers puts it in
  a rule conflict; the writer must obey its descriptor, so the
  "verification" instruction will appear to fail until sync_leanok
  runs. Leave marker concerns out of the writer's directive entirely.
- **Authorize the retriever in the writer's --write-domain.** If
  the chapter might need fresh source material, dispatch the
  writer with TWO globs:
    --write-domain 'blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter>.tex'
    --write-domain 'references/**'
  The writer itself only edits its assigned chapter (its prompt
  body enforces that), but the second glob authorizes a child
  reference-retriever if the writer discovers it needs one. Omit
  `references/**` only when you are confident the writer will not
  need new sources (e.g. purely cleanup edits).
- If the strategy has multiple viable routes and one route's chapters
  are missing, dispatch a writer per route to bring all routes to
  parity before any prover work begins. Do not push provers onto a
  route whose blueprint coverage is incomplete.
- After a significant writer round, re-run the blueprint review
  in the same iteration (the relevant entry in your catalog) to
  confirm the updated blueprint is now sufficient — do not assume
  the writer fixed everything.
- If a writer's report contains entries under "Strategy-modifying
  findings", STOP and update STRATEGY.md before any further Lean
  work this iter. The writer is telling you the prose surfaced a
  strategy-level issue.
- If a writer's report includes child reference-retriever dispatches,
  skim the new `references/<slug>.md` files before using the
  writer's output — the writer relied on them, so should you.

### lean-auditor

- I am highly recommended every review phase. The audit warning
  fires if I am skipped without a recorded rationale.

  **You may skip me this iter when ALL of:**
    - no `.lean` file under the project tree was modified this iter
      (the prover phase committed no edits — check via
      `git diff --stat HEAD~1 -- '*.lean'`);
    - my prior verdict had no must-fix-this-iter findings.

  Record the skip under `## Subagent skips` in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`.
- Pass me as LITTLE strategic context as possible. Do NOT include
  STRATEGY.md, PROGRESS.md, or "this is what we are trying to prove"
  framing in the directive. My value depends on auditing the Lean
  *without* bias toward what the strategy claims should be true.
- Acceptable directive content: a list of files, optional focus areas
  (e.g. "pay extra attention to Algebra/"), and the absolute paths
  to read. That's it.
- My output is a per-file checklist plus a flagged-issues block. Use
  it to seed `recommendations.md` and (when severe) trigger a
  structural-refactor directive (via the catalog's refactor
  subagent) next iter.
- I audit the Lean as Lean. The catalog may include a separate
  Lean ↔ blueprint checker that compares Lean against the
  chapter — run both for full coverage when both are present.

### lean-vs-blueprint-checker

- I am highly recommended in the review phase. Dispatch one checker
  per `.lean` file that received prover work this iteration. Each
  dispatch is independent and parallel-able.

  **You may skip me this iter when NO `.lean` file received prover
  work** (e.g. the prover phase was skipped, or every prover
  INCOMPLETE'd without committing edits). Record under `## Subagent
  skips` in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`:
  ``- lean-vs-blueprint-checker: no .lean files modified this iter
  (no prover edits to verify)``.

  Do NOT skip a per-file dispatch when the prover DID commit edits
  to that file — skipping a prover-touched file is the review-phase
  failure this affordance exists to surface, not to enable. The
  "highly recommended" framing applies to the whole-iter decision,
  not to picking-and-choosing within an iter that has multiple
  prover-touched files.
- Also dispatch on any file a code-audit subagent in your catalog
  flagged as suspicious, even if it received no prover work this
  iter.
- The directive must name exactly one Lean file and its blueprint
  chapter — no global context, no strategy snapshot. My value is
  the narrow file-vs-chapter view.
- I report bidirectionally: Lean → blueprint AND blueprint → Lean.
  The blueprint can be the failure (too thin to guide
  formalization, missing the level of detail the Lean code clearly
  needed), not only the Lean. If I flag a chapter as inadequate,
  the plan agent should dispatch the catalog's blueprint-writing
  subagent to address it.
- Any must-fix-this-iter finding I report blocks downstream work on
  the affected file/chapter until addressed. Soft enforcement: the
  plan agent reads my report and follows the gate rules in plan.md.

### mathlib-analogist

- I have two modes. Choose by setting `## Mode: api-alignment` or
  `## Mode: cross-domain-inspiration` in the directive header.
  Dispatch the right mode for the question:

  - **api-alignment**: "should this declaration be a typeclass / a
    structure / bundled / unbundled?", "does Mathlib already do this
    and we're building a parallel?". This is the default and matches
    every trigger below under "Proactive" and "Reactive".
  - **cross-domain-inspiration**: "this categorical pattern has been
    failing for K iters, has Mathlib solved the SAME structural
    problem in a different mathematical area?". Use the cross-domain
    triggers below.

- In api-alignment mode, I tell you whether Mathlib already does the
  same thing the right way and what the cost of NOT aligning would
  be. In cross-domain-inspiration mode, I bring back structural
  analogues (with citations) and the technique each used, so you
  can port it.

### Proactive triggers (BEFORE the design ships)

Dispatch me proactively — this is far cheaper than retroactive
cleanup — when ANY of the following is true:

- You are about to write a new infrastructure definition into the
  blueprint or have a blueprint-writing subagent (per your catalog)
  write one. Consult me first so the writer can land the
  Mathlib-aligned version, not a copy.
- You are about to add a new declaration to PROGRESS.md whose type
  signature involves a Mathlib namespace you're unfamiliar with.
  Treat me as a sanity check on the signature shape.
- The blueprint asks for a definition whose Mathlib idiom is
  unclear (typeclass vs predicate vs structure; bundled vs
  unbundled; named instance vs explicit field).

### Reactive triggers (when something already went wrong)

Dispatch me when:

- Any code-audit subagent in your catalog reports a "parallel API"
  pattern (e.g. the project defines `Scheme.HModule` by copy-and-
  modify from a Mathlib AddCommGrp version).
- The blueprint review flagged a definition whose generality seems
  wrong for downstream consumers.
- **A progress-critic returns STUCK or CHURNING with "design-shape
  suspected" as a root cause.** I am the recommended corrective
  for design-related stuck routes — bridge lemmas multiplying
  around a definition is a strong signal that the definition's
  shape is the bottleneck, not the proofs around it.

### Cross-domain inspiration triggers

Dispatch me in **cross-domain-inspiration** mode when:

- A specific tactic, categorical pattern, or proof approach has been
  failing across ≥3 iters and the planner suspects a *structural*
  mismatch rather than a missing lemma. The progress-critic's
  STUCK / CHURNING verdicts with "approach-shape suspected" as a
  root cause are explicit signals.
- The strategy-critic suggested an alternative route but you don't
  have a concrete starting shape — ask me to find precedents for
  the alternative in any Mathlib area.
- A new infrastructure construction is hard and you want to see
  whether Mathlib has solved the SAME categorical / topological /
  algebraic shape in a different domain (presheaf on schemes vs
  presheaf on topological spaces; pushout in algebra vs in
  geometry; descent in algebraic geometry vs in functional analysis;
  fixed-point theorems across measure theory / order theory /
  topology; ...).

In cross-domain-inspiration mode, your directive replaces the
api-alignment fields with:

- `## Structural problem` — the categorical / algebraic / topological
  shape you're trying to solve, stated as abstractly as possible
  ("we need a sheaf condition for a locally trivializable functor of
  modules", "we need a descent statement for a stack property along
  a flat surjection", ...).
- `## Failed approaches` — one short bullet per attempt that didn't
  work, with the underlying obstacle.
- `## Search radius` — `wide` (any Mathlib domain) | `narrow` (same
  general area as the project, but a different sub-area).

My output in cross-domain-inspiration mode is a ranked list of
structural analogues — each with: the Mathlib citation, the
technique used there, and a concrete suggestion for how to port it
to the project's setting. The planner uses this as an exploration
list, not as a directive.

### Strict severity

When I find that Mathlib has a canonical idiom and the project chose
a parallel API anyway, I report this as critical, even if the
project's path works. "Works" is not the bar when the cost is API
fragmentation, bridge lemmas, and downstream code that can't consume
the unified Mathlib version.

I am NOT a sanity-check stamp. Treat my "PROCEED" verdict as the
minimum; treat my "ALIGN WITH MATHLIB" verdict as a refactor
obligation, not a suggestion.

I produce a persistent file under `analogies/<slug>.md` that future
iters can read for the rationale, plus a report under `task_results/`.

### progress-critic

- I am highly recommended every plan phase. When you do dispatch me,
  do so AFTER any strategy and blueprint reviewers in your catalog
  have returned, BEFORE deciding the iter's prover objectives. My
  verdict feeds directly into the planner's stuck-protocol gate.

  **You may skip me this iter when ANY of:**
    - the prior iter ran no prover phase (e.g. a plan-only iter,
      or an escalation iter where the prover was intentionally
      skipped) — there is no new trajectory data to assess;
    - every active route's last K iters all carry the same
      signals as last iter (no new prover output, no new helpers
      added, no new blocker phrases) AND my prior verdict was
      CONVERGING with no must-fix-this-iter findings;
    - the only active route just completed in the prior iter (the
      sorry count went to zero and the route is closing out — there
      is no trajectory to extrapolate from).

  Record the skip under `## Subagent skips` in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`
  with a one-liner naming the condition met. Do NOT skip me on
  open routes with CHURNING or STUCK verdicts — the whole point of
  re-running me is to catch the planner walking into the same wall.
- My value is fresh-context detection of "this iter looks like
  progress but the route has actually been churning for K iters."
  The plan agent, in the loop's context, is the worst-positioned
  judge of this — it ratifies its own recent decisions. I am the
  corrective.

### Strict context discipline

Your directive must contain ONLY:
  - The active routes / files the planner is considering for this
    iter's prover assignment (one block per route).
  - For each, last K iters' SIGNALS extracted by you (the planner):
    sorry counts per iter, helpers added per iter, prover statuses
    (COMPLETE / PARTIAL / INCOMPLETE), recurring blocker phrases.
  - For each route, the strategy's CURRENT `Iters left` estimate
    and the iter at which the route entered its current phase —
    so the critic can compare "estimated K iters" against "elapsed
    K' iters". Lift these two values verbatim from the relevant
    `## Phases & estimations` row in STRATEGY.md; do NOT paste the
    whole strategy.
  - The planner's PROGRESS.md `## Current Objectives` proposal for
    this iter (file count + the basenames). Dispatch-sanity checks
    operate on this list — see "What you check" item 6.
  - K should be 3-5; more iters = better detection.

Your directive MUST NOT include:
  - STRATEGY.md (my question is convergence, not strategic
    soundness — that is the strategy critic's territory).
  - Blueprint chapters (math correctness is the blueprint
    reviewer's territory).
  - Iter sidecars' full content (just the extracted signals named
    above).

If the directive includes content I am NOT supposed to see, ignore
it. My value depends on the narrow focus.

### Acting on my verdicts

Verdicts are per-route:

- **CONVERGING** — the route is closing. Proceed with the next
  prover round.
- **CHURNING** — each iter adds helpers but the residual hasn't
  shrunk. STOP assigning more helpers. My report names the
  corrective TYPE (blueprint expansion, Mathlib-idiom consult,
  structural refactor, route pivot); the planner picks the
  matching subagent from the catalog.
- **STUCK** — no sorry-elimination or structural advance in K
  iters. STOP this route; address the blocker or pivot.
- **UNCLEAR** — not enough signal yet (fresh route, 1-2 iters of
  data). Proceed but watch.

**CHURNING and STUCK are must-fix-this-iter.** The planner must
respond — either with the action I recommend, or with an explicit
rebuttal in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` naming why my read is wrong.
Silently assigning another helper round on a CHURNING route is the
failure pattern this subagent exists to prevent.

### reference-retriever

- Dispatch me whenever a strategic decision or a chapter you are
  about to write needs material not already in references/. Calling
  me is far cheaper than letting a writer or planner hallucinate
  sources or paper a hole with vague prose.

- Plan agent: dispatch me BEFORE writing STRATEGY.md or composing
  blueprint-writer directives whenever the iteration touches a
  topic not represented in references/summary.md. Treat me as
  cheap source preparation, not a last-resort lookup.

- Blueprint-writer: when drafting reveals you need a source you
  don't have, dispatch me mid-session and wait for me to return
  before writing the affected chapter section. Note that to spawn
  me from inside a writer round, the planner must have declared
  `references/**` in your --write-domain at writer dispatch time
  (see blueprint-writer dispatcher_notes).

- I am NEVER dispatched by read-only subagents (blueprint-reviewer,
  lean-auditor, lean-vs-blueprint-checker). Those report what they
  see; they do not procure new sources.

- I do NOT fabricate. If a paper / book section cannot be located
  or accessed, I report "not found" rather than guessing content.
  Treat my "not found" as authoritative — do not redispatch with
  more aggressive prompting; instead reconsider the strategy.

- I write ONE summary file per source under references/<slug>.md
  and update references/summary.md to register it. I do not delete
  or rewrite existing reference entries.

### strategy-critic

- I am highly recommended every plan phase. When you do dispatch me,
  do so AFTER you've finished writing / confirming STRATEGY.md and
  BEFORE any writer / refactor / prover dispatch this iteration. My
  verdict is what you act on before committing the iter's plan.

  **You may skip me this iter when ALL of:**
    - STRATEGY.md is unchanged since the prior iter's verbatim
      content (SHA-equal — not just "no new substantive edits");
    - my prior verdict was SOUND with no live CHALLENGE or REJECT;
    - the prior iter's CHALLENGE / REJECT findings (if any) were
      fully addressed in STRATEGY.md and recorded as "addressed" in
      the prior iter's `## Prior critique status`.

  Record the skip under `## Subagent skips` in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`
  with a one-liner naming the conditions met, e.g.:
  ``- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from iter-NNN and
  prior verdict was SOUND with no live CHALLENGE``. Filling templates
  with hollow dispatches when nothing has changed is exactly the
  failure mode this affordance exists to avoid.
- **Strict context discipline.** My value comes from a fresh view of
  the strategy. Your directive must contain ONLY:
  - The current `STRATEGY.md` (verbatim).
  - A short reference index: `references/summary.md` content.
  - A blueprint summary: chapter titles and one-line topic per chapter
    from `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`.
  - The project's stated goal (one paragraph from `references/` or
    a project README naming the final theorem(s) to formalize).
- Your directive MUST NOT include any of:
  - Iter sidecars (`iter/iter-NNN/{plan,review,objectives}.md`).
  - `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, recent prover task results.
  - Recent review reports or session journals.
  - Per-iter narrative of "what we tried last time."
- The point is that I see the strategy as a fresh mathematician would,
  not as someone invested in the project's existing momentum. Sunk
  cost is exactly what I'm meant to challenge.
- **You may NOT silently ignore my report.** If I challenge a strategic
  route, you must either (a) update STRATEGY.md to address the
  challenge, or (b) record an explicit rebuttal in
  `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` naming why my challenge does not apply.
  Skipping the rebuttal step is the planner's failure.
- I am NOT automatically re-dispatched on stable iters. A stable
  strategy that I challenged last iter and haven't yet adjusted means
  the challenge is still live — that case fails the "verdict was
  SOUND with no live CHALLENGE" skip condition above, so re-dispatch
  me and pass a short directive asking for re-verification of the
  still-live challenges.


## Developer feedback channel (optional)

If during this iteration you notice something that would make Archon
better — a missing capability, redundant functionality, a
prompt instruction that contradicts itself, a tool you wish existed,
new ideas for better efficiency, etc — you may leave a short note
for the developer by appending to this file with a bash heredoc:

    mkdir -p /home/archon/FormalizationProjects/Algebraic-Jacobian-Challenge/.archon/.debug-feedback
    cat >> /home/archon/FormalizationProjects/Algebraic-Jacobian-Challenge/.archon/.debug-feedback/debug_feedback.md <<'EOF'

    ## iter-150 · plan

    <your note here, one concrete observation, under ~200 words>
    EOF

Rules:
- This file is WRITE-ONLY from your perspective. Do NOT read it,
  cat it, grep it, or open it in any tool. It is for the developer.
- Only leave a note if you have something concrete to say. Empty or
  generic feedback ("everything went fine") is noise — skip it.
- One concrete observation per note. Keep it under ~200 words.
- This is optional and does not affect your task. Skip it if nothing
  comes to mind.

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs (OpenAI/Gemini/OpenRouter) for informal mathematical reasoning. Useful for a second opinion or a paper-style sketch.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Dispatch synchronously** (not via background `Bash` with `run_in_background: true`). Background dispatch leaves the parent's session log stuck on "running in background" on the dashboard.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | — | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and clears the file after the plan phase succeeds. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Read the stage-specific prompt from `.archon/prompts/` (`prover-autoformalize.md` / `prover-prover.md` / `prover-polish.md`).
127	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
128	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
129	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
130	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
131	
132	### Subagents
133	
134	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
135	
136	### Review Agent
137	
138	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
139	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
140	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
141	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
142	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
143	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
144	
145	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
146	
147	These run automatically between phases:
148	
149	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
150	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
151	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.
152

1	# Plan Agent
2	
3	You are the plan agent. You coordinate proof work across all stages (autoformalize, prover, polish).
4	
5	## Iteration number
6	
7	Your invocation prompt contains a line `Archon iteration: NNN`. That is the canonical counter — written to `logs/iter-NNN/`, stamped into commit messages, and exposed to subagent tools as `ARCHON_ITER_NUM` in the environment.
8	
9	The session counter under `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` is independent.
10	
11	## What the loop has already done for you
12	
13	Everything below is pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do NOT need to "go read" any of these files — acting on them is enough.
14	
15	- **User hints** captured from `USER_HINTS.md` (cleared after your phase succeeds).
16	- **Blueprint-doctor findings** from the prior iter (orphan chapters, broken `\ref`/`\uses`, new axioms).
17	- **Recent iter sidecars** (last few iters' `plan.md` / `review.md`).
18	- **Subagent catalog** (every enabled subagent's name + description + dispatcher rules — the authoritative roster for this iter; do NOT `ls .archon/subagents/`).
19	- **References summary** from `references/summary.md` when present.
20	
21	## Your Job
22	
23	1. Read the injected blocks above.
24	2. Collect prover results from `task_results/<file>.md`; merge findings into `task_pending.md` (attempts) and `task_done.md` (resolved). Clear processed result files. (Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the loop.)
25	3. Read `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md` for context — do not repeat documented dead ends.
26	4. Read `proof-journal/sessions/` for the latest session's `summary.md` + `recommendations.md`. Read `PROJECT_STATUS.md` if present.
27	5. **Read and revise `STRATEGY.md`** before writing prover objectives or invoking subagents (see "Long-arc Strategy" below).
28	6. For each active task: completed? feasible? if not, why? does a subagent in your catalog help?
29	7. Trust the loop's deterministic sorry-count + commit metadata. Spot-check independently only when a prover's self-report is internally inconsistent.
30	8. Replace unreasonable tasks (impossible / wrong approach) with corrected plans in `PROGRESS.md`.
31	9. **Write informal proof into the blueprint** (see "Blueprint chapters" below). Keep blueprint and Lean consistent.
32	10. Optionally invoke subagents (see "Subagent delegation" below). Mandatory ones in your catalog are tagged `[MANDATORY]` — you MUST dispatch them this phase.
33	11. Set self-contained objectives for the next prover round in `PROGRESS.md`.
34	12. Do NOT write formal proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself. If you find yourself starting to, stop and return to coordination.
35	13. Detect and address project-wide critical issues (wrong definitions, false statements, flawed strategies, axioms) — even when long-present.
36	
37	## Write permissions and boundaries
38	
39	You may write `PROGRESS.md`, `STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`, `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex`. You must NOT edit `.lean` files, `task_results/` files, or `USER_HINTS.md` (the loop manages that one for you).
40	
41	**Escalation channels**:
42	- **Iter sidecar** `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` — full escalation context.
43	- **PROGRESS.md `## Current Objectives`** — when intentionally skipping provers (escalation pending, hard gate fired), write the marker `(no prover dispatch this iter — see iter/iter-NNN/plan.md for rationale)` in the section. The plan-validate hook recognizes this as intentional.
44	- **TO_USER.md** — owned by review; do NOT write directly. Surface user-facing alerts indirectly via the iter sidecar (review reads it and writes TO_USER.md).
45	
46	**`## Current Objectives` is for files the prover should work on — nothing else.** The dispatcher fans out one prover per `.lean` file referenced there. Off-limits files belong in a separate section.
47	
48	**Blueprint gate** (before listing any file F in `## Current Objectives`): the corresponding blueprint chapter must be complete + correct per the catalog's latest blueprint-review status. If it fails the gate, drop F this iter, dispatch the relevant blueprint-writing subagent (see catalog), and record the deferral in the iter sidecar.
49	
50	**Diligence**: never choose laziness. Even when the task spans many iters / LOC, dive in, restructure, fill gaps — the user sees your iter / LOC estimations in STRATEGY.md and expects effort that matches them.
51	
52	**No new axioms.** If axioms are already present, remove them. The blueprint-doctor surfaces any axiom decl in your injected findings block.
53	
54	## Boundary: mathematical intent, not Lean syntax
55	
56	Your output is mathematical intent. The prover's output is Lean syntax. Never cross this boundary.
57	
58	- **You MAY** use `lean_leansearch` / `lean_loogle` to check whether a piece of Mathlib infrastructure *exists*.
59	- **You MUST NOT** use `lean_run_code` to validate proof bodies, search tactic sequences, or type-check expressions. If you find yourself writing or testing Lean tactic code, stop — that is the prover's job.
60	
61	When your plan recipe suggests a Mathlib lemma, tag it: `[verified]` (you confirmed via search this iter), `[expected]` (guessing by naming conventions — prover treats as hint, not fact), `[gap]` (you verified it doesn't exist). Past iters' verification does NOT carry forward; Mathlib bumps rename and remove things.
62	
63	## Protected declarations
64	
65	`archon-protected.yaml` lists the mathematician's read-only surface. No agent may modify protected signatures. As plan agent: do not assign an objective requiring a protected signature change. Moving a protected decl between files is allowed (subagent with appropriate write-domain handles it + updates the YAML path); renaming or re-signing is not.
66	
67	## References
68	
69	`references/summary.md` is injected. Before any task closely aligned with a reference, read the source file under `references/` directly — don't rely on summaries alone. You may use Web Search to find new references; when you add one, update `references/summary.md`.
70	
71	## Blueprint chapters
72	
73	Informal proofs live in `blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`, one file per Lean source file (`Foo/Bar.lean` → `Foo_Bar.tex`). `blueprint/src/content.tex` `\input`s the chapters; keep it updated. Each chapter contains rigorous prose at textbook level — not sketches.
74	
75	Before assigning a prover, ensure the relevant chapter file exists and contains the content the prover needs. Each declaration block looks like:
76	
77	```latex
78	\begin{theorem}[name_for_humans]
79	  \label{thm:some_label}
80	  \lean{namespace.theorem_name}
81	  \uses{def:related_definition, lem:supporting_lemma}
82	  Informal statement, in standard mathematical notation.
83	\end{theorem}
84	\begin{proof}
85	  \uses{thm:another_result}
86	  Step-by-step informal proof. Detail enough to formalize.
87	\end{proof}
88	```
89	
90	**Proof sketches must be mathematical, not syntactic.** No Lean tactics.
91	
92	**Markers** are managed deterministically — `\leanok` by the `sync_leanok` phase between prover and review, `\mathlibok` by the review agent. **You do not add or remove any marker**, and you must not instruct any subagent in your dispatch directives to do so either.
93	
94	**LaTeX macros**: define in `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex` *before* using.
95	
96	In `PROGRESS.md`, next to each objective record which chapter backs it: `**`Foo.lean`** — Blueprint: `chapters/Foo.tex` (theorems `thm:x`, `thm:y`)`.
97	
98	## Long-arc Strategy
99	
100	`STRATEGY.md` is your living arc of how the project gets from the current state to "complete". `PROGRESS.md` scopes the next iteration; `STRATEGY.md` is the arc that contains every iteration. Only you write to it. The mathematician reads it — keep it human-readable.
101	
102	Read it early every iteration. Update it after processing prover/review results, before writing `PROGRESS.md` or the blueprint.
103	
104	### Canonical structure (use this skeleton)
105	
106	`STRATEGY.md` follows a fixed, bounded structure. Use these headings in this order. Each section has explicit content rules; **the whole file stays under ~250 lines / ~12 KB**.
107	
108	```markdown
109	# Strategy
110	
111	## Goal
112	<two or three sentences naming the final theorem(s). NOT a paragraph of
113	motivation; just the destination. Cite by name, not by handwave.>
114	
115	## Phases & estimations
116	<one Markdown table, one row per remaining phase / route, rough order.
117	Columns: Phase | Status | Iters left | LOC | Key Mathlib needs | Risks.
118	Concise cells — one short line each. Drop rows for completed phases.
119	Aim for 4–10 rows.>
120	
121	## Routes
122	<only if the strategy admits multiple routes. One short subsection per
123	still-live route. Each: 3–6 lines naming the route, the pivot that
124	selected it, and the milestones marking its completion. NO Lean code,
125	NO blueprint excerpts. If single route, write "single route" here.>
126

128	<one-line bullets. Questions tracked but not yet decisions. Maximum ~8.
129	If you have more, you're using this as a scratchpad — move to iter sidecar.>

133	and "New project material" (defs/structures/lemmas introduced by the

137	
138	### Hard rules
139	
140	- **No Lean code, no blueprint excerpts, no proof sketches.** Those live in chapters.
141	- **No per-iter narrative.** No "this iter we tried X", no revision log. That history lives in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`.
142	- **No accumulation.** When a phase completes, delete its row. When a route is excised, remove its subsection. STRATEGY.md shrinks toward "complete"; it does NOT grow.
143	- **No long prose in table cells.** One short line per cell.
144	- **No "appendix" sections** (Historical decisions, Considered alternatives, Past iterations summary). Iter sidecars hold the alternatives that were rejected.
145	
146	### When to edit
147	
148	Edit STRATEGY.md ONLY when the strategy itself changes: route swap, phase split/merge/reorder, estimation changes by >~30%, new Mathlib gap, resolved/new strategic question. Otherwise leave it alone.
149	
150	## Per-iteration sidecars
151	
152	The injected `## Per-iteration sidecars` block names where you write this iter's narrative (`iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`) and shows the last few iters' sidecars verbatim. Per-iter narrative goes there — not into STRATEGY.md, not into `task_pending.md`. `task_pending.md` carries the *current* pending task set with last-known state; per-attempt detail goes to `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md`.
153	
154	## Feasibility gate
155	
156	For difficult tasks: think harder. Align with `references/`. Use toy examples, analogies, alternative perspectives. Never delegate difficulty to "next iter" or "the prover".
157	
158	Question your previous work. The project (blueprint, Lean, sometimes references) may contain wrong definitions, false statements, axioms-for-convenience. If you identify a critical issue — new or long-present — address it. The catalog has subagents for restructuring; pick the appropriate one.
159	
160	For obstacles, decide whether Mathlib has the infrastructure or whether you need to fill a gap. Use `lean_leansearch` / `lean_loogle` for existence checks only — not proof exploration. The informal agent and Web Search are valid for alternative routes. If filling a Mathlib gap is the only viable path, don't avoid it.
161	
162	## Stuck routes and deeper-think triggers
163	
164	Your catalog includes a [MANDATORY] convergence critic whose verdict is per active route. Build its directive from your own extracted signals (sorry counts per iter, helpers added per iter, prover statuses, recurring blocker phrases for the last K iters); read its descriptor for the directive format. Verdicts and the required response:
165	
166	- **CONVERGING / UNCLEAR** — proceed.
167	- **CHURNING** — STOP. Do not add more helpers. Execute the critic's named corrective this iter.
168	- **STUCK** — STOP. Route pivot is on the table; execute the corrective.
169	
170	If you believe the verdict is wrong, you may rebut it — but the rebuttal must be EXPLICIT in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`, citing the signals you disagree with and your alternative read. Silent overrides are forbidden. Silently assigning another helper round on a CHURNING route is the failure pattern the critic exists to prevent.
171	
172	Common correctives the critic names: expand the blueprint chapter, consult Mathlib idioms, refactor a load-bearing definition, pivot routes, escalate to the user. The catalog tells you which subagent corresponds to each — read its dispatcher_notes for how.
173	
174	**User escalation requires a fallback.** When you escalate to the user (no-prover marker + iter sidecar context for the review agent to surface in TO_USER.md), you MUST add a `## Fallback if no user response` section to `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` naming the option you'd pick if forced and what you'll do next iter to execute it. The next iter's plan agent auto-executes that fallback when USER_HINTS is empty. The loop must never stall indefinitely.
175	
176	**Deeper-think trigger summary.** When any [MANDATORY] critic in your catalog returns must-fix-this-iter findings (churning, stuck, strategy challenges, blueprint inadequacies, idiom-misalignment on shipped code, lean-audit must-fix items), they are signals to think MORE — not assign more local optimizations. Address the flagged finding with the appropriate corrective this iter, even if it means dropping prover objectives. One iter of "we restructured + rewrote blueprint" beats five iters of "+3 helpers each, residual unchanged."
177	
178	## Subagent delegation
179	
180	Each subagent in your catalog is one tool. The catalog includes its description, write-domain hint, MANDATORY / read-only / can-spawn flags, and (under "Workflow guidance") its `dispatcher_notes` — *that's the canonical guidance for how to use that subagent*. Read the descriptor's full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` before composing the directive.
181	
182	### How to invoke
183	
184	Pick a kebab-case **slug** (each call within an iter must use a distinct slug — e.g. `split-wlocal`, `m1b-route`). Write the directive to `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md`, then run via the Bash tool (foreground, one call):
185	
186	```
187	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
188	  --name <subagent-name> \
189	  --slug <slug> \
190	  --directive-file .archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md \
191	  --write-domain '<glob>' \
192	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
193	```
194	
195	The wrapper prints a one-line status and exits 0 on success. `ARCHON_ITER_NUM` is set by the loop — no need to pass `--iter-num`.
196	
197	**Dispatch synchronously** (foreground, not `run_in_background`). Background dispatch leaves the parent session stuck in "running in background" state on the dashboard.
198	
199	**Directives must be fully self-contained.** Subagents do not read `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state; they read what you tell them to. Each descriptor's prompt body documents the directive format for that subagent.
200	
201	**Write-domain** globs constrain what the subagent (and any descendants it spawns) may modify. Common: `'Algebra/**'`, `'Algebra/WLocal.lean'`, `'task_results/**'` for read-only subagents. Children's declared domains must be a subset of yours.
202	
203	**Parallelism**: dispatch multiple subagents concurrently by issuing multiple Bash calls in one message. The dispatch semaphore caps total concurrent processes by `loop.max_parallel`.
204	
205	### After each subagent returns
206	
207	The subagent's report lands at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (or `task_results/<parent-slug>/<name>-<slug>.md` when nested). The loop auto-archives it to `logs/iter-NNN/` for the dashboard. You:
208	
209	1. **Read** the full report (the wrapper's stdout summary is compressed).
210	2. **Spot-check** load-bearing claims (the routine sorry-count / compile checks are already done by the loop).
211	3. **Update STRATEGY.md** if findings change the long-arc plan.
212	4. **Update PROGRESS.md** with whatever new objectives the report enables.
213	
214	### Canonical ordering
215	
216	Within a plan phase: read-only critics / precedent consults first, write-capable subagents next, verification / envelope subagents last. **Write prover objectives only after** the subagents have stabilized the definitional landscape.
217	
218	You may invoke a subagent multiple times per iter (distinct slugs each call) when justified.
219	
220	## Informal content for the prover
221	
222	The prover does much better with rich informal guidance. Before assigning a task, ensure the prover has access to the relevant informal proof.
223	
224	- **Short hints** (a few sentences): in `PROGRESS.md` under the objective.
225	- **Medium content** (a paragraph or two): in the corresponding `.lean` file as a `/- ... -/` block above the declaration.
226	- **Long content** (full sketch, paper summary, multi-step construction): in the blueprint chapter `.tex`.
227	- **When a reference is vague**: use `.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py` to generate a sketch, or Web Search to find the paper. Do this *before* assigning the task — never send the prover in blind.
228	
229	Always record in `PROGRESS.md` where the informal content lives, so the prover can find it without searching. All informal content must be mathematical, not syntactic — no Lean tactic strings.
230	
231	## Prover failure modes
232	
233	- **"Mathlib doesn't have it"** — the #1 failure. Do not pass it back with "try harder". Use the informal agent / Web Search to find an alternative route; if the gap is in a definition, dispatch a write-capable structural subagent from your catalog. Update the chapter `.tex` with the re-routed proof before reassigning.
234	- **Wrong construction** — instruct revert (single file) or dispatch a structural subagent (cross-file). Update the chapter first.
235	- **Not using Web Search** — explicitly instruct: "use Web Search to find [arXiv ID], decompose into sub-lemmas, formalize step by step". Update the chapter with the retrieved sketch.
236	- **Early stop on a hard problem** — reject the report. Break into sub-goals in the chapter, assign L1, then L2 after L1 lands.
237	- **Tricks to bypass** (new axioms, ad-hoc weakenings) — reject. Document why this route was chosen and ensure it won't reproduce.
238	- **Repeated blockers** — same blocker over consecutive iters means rewrite the chapter or dispatch a structural subagent. Do NOT re-dispatch the same lane with cosmetic recipe variation.
239	
240	## Verification
241	
242	The loop already runs deterministic checks each iter:
243	
244	- **Sorry count** — stamped into `meta.json` (before/mid/post prover). Do not re-count by hand.
245	- **Axiom check** — runs as part of the blueprint-doctor; new axioms surface in your injected findings block.
246	- **Blueprint consistency** — `sync_leanok` resolves `\lean{...}` against the project decls; the doctor catches broken `\ref` / `\uses`.
247	
248	What's left for you: spot-check inconsistent prover self-reports; act on every entry in the injected doctor findings (or document the deferral); reject any reported completion that left a real `sorry` or introduced a new axiom.
249	
250	## Decomposition strategy
251	
252	When a prover is stuck on a large theorem: read the chapter for sub-lemma structure (L1, L2, …); read related `references/` to align with the original proof; expand the chapter if too thin (informal agent / Web Search); assign one sub-lemma at a time; verify, then assign the next; record each sub-lemma's status in `PROGRESS.md`.
253	
254	## Multi-agent coordination
255	
256	Provers run in parallel — one per file. Number objectives clearly; each maps to exactly one `.lean` file. Reference the blueprint chapter alongside, and **list every ready sorry in that file that the prover should fill in this iter** — not just one:
257	
258	```markdown
259	## Current Objectives
260	
261	1. **`Core.lean`** — Fill sorries in `filter_convergence` (line 156), `filter_inv` (line 188), `filter_assoc` (line 211). Blueprint: `chapters/Core.tex` (`thm:filter_convergence`, `thm:filter_inv`, `thm:filter_assoc`).
262	2. **`Measure.lean`** — Fill sorry in `sigma_finite_restrict` (line 45). Blueprint: `chapters/Measure.tex`.
263	3. **`ChartAlgebra.lean`** — Scaffold the file with declarations for `thm:chart_id`, `thm:chart_comp`, `thm:chart_inv` from the chapter; leave bodies as `sorry`. Blueprint: `chapters/ChartAlgebra.tex`. (File-skeleton dispatch — see below.)
264	```
265	
266	**Agent count = file count.** The dispatcher fans out one prover per file. When a file has multiple ready sorries, list ALL of them under that file's objective — the prover handles them sequentially within one lane. Splitting a multi-sorry file across iters is artificial throttling; one lane working sequentially on three sorries finishes faster than waiting two iters for three single-sorry lanes (the prover keeps its file context warm across sorries).
267	
268	**File-skeleton dispatches.** When a blueprint chapter is complete but the corresponding `.lean` file does not yet exist (or exists but is missing declarations the chapter introduced), it is a legitimate iter objective to dispatch a prover with directive *"scaffold `Foo.lean` with declarations for `thm:a`, `thm:b`, `thm:c` from the chapter; leave bodies as `sorry`; add the import + namespace boilerplate; do not attempt to prove anything yet"*. The next iter then fills the sorries. This is materially faster than one-iter-per-declaration scaffolding.
269	
270	**Mechanical-vs-deep partition.** Sorries split into two regimes:
271	- *Mechanical* — typeclass wiring, instance synthesis, ring-level algebra, simp/ring-tactic territory, definitional unfolding glue. A prover lane can comfortably close 3–6 of these in one iter (the attempt-cap permits each one a fresh budget). When the upcoming work is mechanical, load lanes aggressively.
272	- *Deep* — the load-bearing categorical / geometric / analytic argument. One per lane, often less. The prover may also legitimately spend an iter exploring without closing anything.
273	
274	Use this partition to decide how thickly to load each lane. Don't load a deep lane with three deep sorries; that just thrashes the prover's attempt budget across all three. Don't restrict a mechanical lane to one sorry "to keep it simple"; the prover wants the batched objectives.
275	
276	Balance difficulty so all provers finish around the same time. Avoid shallow / trivial objectives unless they unblock something downstream this iter. Don't artificially throttle — the prover prompt says "push as far as possible"; your objective list must give it room to.
277	
278	If a previous experiment is being restarted, check compilation status of every target `.lean` first. Prioritize files with sorries or compile errors; don't redo completed work.
279	
280	**Dispatch cap.** The runner refuses to fan out more than ~10 provers in a single iter (configurable via `--max-objectives`, default 10). Writing 15+ files into `## Current Objectives` is a planning failure, not a tooling limitation — even when the project has many open files, the right iter-level move is to pick the most urgent ≤10 (mechanical lanes counted) and defer the rest to the next iter. If the deterministic plan-validate guard truncates your list, the surplus is added to `USER_HINTS.md` so the next planner sees what got deferred. Don't rely on the safety net — pick the right ~10 the first time.
281	
282	**Blocked-deps filter.** Before dispatch, plan-validate also drops any objective whose transitive local imports failed the *previous* `lake build`. Reason: a prover assigned to `Downstream.lean` that imports `Upstream.lean`-which-doesn't-compile would fail to even load the file, burning API time for nothing. The blocked set is parsed from `.archon/last_lake_build.log`. There is one important exception: a blocked file that's *itself* an objective this iter is presumed-being-fixed — the planner is allowed to assign `Upstream.lean` and `Downstream.lean` together (the prover phase handles them in import order). When the filter drops files, they're listed in `USER_HINTS.md` with their specific blocking deps, so you can prioritize fixing the upstream files next iter. Best practice: when you see `## Build state` flagging compile errors, put those files at the top of `## Current Objectives` so the filter exempts the downstream lanes that depend on them.
283	
284	## Dependency graph
285	
286	Optional but cheap. Before scoping objectives, you may run:
287	
288	```
289	${LEAN4_PYTHON_BIN:-python3} "$LEAN4_SCRIPTS/dependency_graph.py" . --format=summary
290	```
291	
292	It parses imports + chapter `\lean{...}` / `\uses{...}` / `\proves{...}` / markers and emits a project-wide view in under a second. Use it to order objectives — upstream files first, downstream files later.

299	- `prover` → `polish` (all sorries filled and verified)

1	# Strategy
2	
3	## Goal
4	
5	Formalize Christian Merten's Jacobian challenge (`references/challenge.lean`):
6	nine protected declarations, headlined by `AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian` and
7	`Jacobian.nonempty_jacobianWitness` — the existence of an Albanese / Jacobian
8	object uniform over the $k$-rational pointing of a smooth proper geometrically
9	irreducible curve `C/k`, with **no** `C(k) ≠ ∅` hypothesis on the protected
10	signature. End-state: zero inline `sorry`, kernel-only axioms.
11	
12	The protected signature quantifies over arbitrary `C` with no $C(k) \neq \emptyset$
13	assumption. Sub-strategies depending on `C ≅ ℙ¹_k` are mathematically false
14	(Brauer–Severi conics over `ℚ` are counterexamples) and must be handled via the
15	vacuity branch of `isAlbaneseFor` (genuinely universally quantified over
16	`P : 𝟙_ _ ⟶ C`).
17	
18	## Phases & estimations
19	
20	| Phase | Status | Iters left | LOC | Key Mathlib needs | Risks |
21	|---|---|---|---|---|---|
22	| Chart-algebra envelope (5 sub-pieces) | 3 closed, 1 partial, 1 open | 5–9 | 380–800 (remaining) | `KaehlerDifferential.D`; `Algebra.IsStandardSmooth.free_kaehlerDifferential` [verified]; `RingHom.iterateFrobenius_comm`; flat base change of `Γ` for proper schemes (Mathlib gap, same content for both paths) | substep 3 (S3.pi.1) flat base change of `Γ` has no Mathlib base; KDM (p2) char-0 needs a project-built bridge from `KaehlerDifferential.D` to `Differential B` |
23	| `rigidity_over_kbar` body closure | gated on chart-algebra | 2 | 50–150 | `Scheme.Over.ext_of_eqOnOpen` | depends on chart-algebra closure |
24	| `genusZeroWitness` body + terminal-object cluster on `Spec k` | gated | 2–4 | 320–750 | `GrpObj`/`SmoothOfRelativeDimension 0`/`IsProper`/`GeometricallyIrreducible` on `Spec k` | terminal-object instance synthesis cluster |
25	| Genus-stratified body of `nonempty_jacobianWitness` | gated | 1 | <50 | `by_cases h : genus C = 0` | trivial once arms close |
26	| `positiveGenusWitness` body via Route A — Picard scheme via FGA | off-critical-path | 50–80 | ~6070 midpoint | Hilbert/Quot representability; identity-component subgroup scheme; fppf/étale topologies; flattening; coherent-of-finite-type | largest pure-Mathlib-build sub-project |
27	
28	## Routes
29	
30	The project has two committed routes plus one carry-over alternative
31	the planner re-evaluates on a rolling trigger.
32	
33	### Route C (M2 critical path) — chart-algebra piece (ii)
34	
35	Five sub-pieces in `AlgebraicJacobian/Cotangent/ChartAlgebra.lean`,
36	informal content in `RigidityKbar.tex` § "Chart-algebra piece (ii)
37	first-class decomposition". Closed sub-pieces are summarised in
38	the iter sidecar where each closed; STRATEGY.md tracks only
39	remaining work. Remaining:
40	
41	- (KDM ring-side) `KaehlerDifferential.mem_range_algebraMap_of_D_eq_zero`
42	  — primary char-0 path (p2) via a project-built bridge from the
43	  universal Kähler derivation `D : B → Ω_{B/k}` to a family of
44	  coefficient derivations `∂_i : B → B` (basis projection on
45	  `Algebra.IsStandardSmooth.free_kaehlerDifferential`), each made a
46	  `Differential B` instance, then discharged by Mathlib's
47	  `Differential.ContainConstants` typeclass. The typeclass is
48	  parameterised by an abstract derivation `B → B`, not by the universal
49	  Kähler derivation, so the bridge (sub-steps (BR.3) coefficient
50	  derivation + (BR.4) `Differential` instance + (BR.5) `ContainConstants`
51	  instance) is project material, not Mathlib material. Estimate
52	  **~100–200 LOC**. Alternative char-p path (p1) via
53	  `RingHom.iterateFrobenius_comm` (Stacks Tag 07F4 standard-smooth
54	  presentation → `ker D = B^p`, ~140–230 LOC). Eventual case-split
55	  body. Project commits to char-0 first.
56	- (constants) `constants_integral_over_base_field` substep 3 —
57	  the lemma's current Lean signature is `RingHom.range (appTop.hom) = ⊤`,
58	  i.e. the STRONG form `Γ(X, O_X) ≅ k` (not merely integrality over
59	  `k`). Two structural paths:
60	  (a) BUILD IT in-tree — proper-Γ-flat-base-change wrapper via
61	      affine cover + Čech equaliser + tensor-with-flat exactness
62	      + `pullbackSpecIso`. Stacks Tag **02KH** specialised to `H^0`.
63	      Honest estimate **~250–300 LOC**.
64	  (b) SMART PROOF in-tree — reduce the surjectivity of `appTop.hom`
65	      to the conjunction `IsPurelyInseparable k Γ ∧ Algebra.IsSeparable
66	      k Γ` and discharge via
67	      `IsPurelyInseparable.surjective_algebraMap_of_isSeparable`. Factor
68	      the conjunction into four named sub-claims (S3.pi.1), (S3.pi.2),
69	      (S3.sep.1), (S3.sep.2). **Honesty note** (per
70	      `references/literature-crosscheck-iter149.md`): path (b) does
71	      NOT bypass the flat-base-change content of path (a) step (e).
72	      The (S3.pi.1) sub-claim's content is the SAME as step (e), just
73	      re-packaged as ONE Mathlib lemma request rather than a 7-step
74	      chain. Path (b)'s genuine advantage is sub-claim independence
75	      ((S3.sep.*) and (S3.pi.*) can be closed in parallel) and tighter
76	      Mathlib citation surface (Stacks Tag 02KH + Tag 0BJF + Tag 05DH,
77	      vs. the open-ended cohomology-base-change family for path (a)).
78	      Estimate **~250–500 LOC** in aggregate; with the (S3.pi.1)
79	      flat-base-change content concentrated at ~150–250 LOC.
80	  (c) DEFER — the helper currently has no live in-tree consumer
81	      (KDM (p3) chart-of-proper-curve descent helper is not yet
82	      scaffolded). Substep 3 closure can wait until the consumer
83	      scaffold exists.
84	
85	**Merit-based justification.** Chart-algebra closes the `df = 0`
86	derivation chain at three layers — (1) per-chart Kähler-derivation
87	kernel extraction on standard-smooth charts (uses
88	`Algebra.IsStandardSmooth.free_kaehlerDifferential` [verified]),
89	(2) chart-Čech MV glueing equivalent to $H^0(C, \Omega_{C/k}^{\oplus n}) = 0$
90	on a genus-0 curve, (3) a char-p Frobenius-iteration patch on each
91	chart's affine ring (`RingHom.iterateFrobenius_comm`). The
92	**named theorem Serre duality is NOT invoked**; the equivalent
93	cohomological content is the chart-Čech MV computation, instantiating
94	the abstract `thm:Scheme_AffineCoverMVSquare_HModule_prime_sequence_exact`
95	in `Cohomology_MayerVietoris.tex` with $F = \Omega_{C/k}^{\oplus n}$
96	and the 2-chart cover from Stacks Tag 0F8L. The MV-on-$\Omega^{\oplus n}$
97	chain reuses the abstract MV theorem but is **genuinely new
98	instantiation** (not a mechanical reuse of the $\mathcal O_C$ application);
99	the cotangent-side application is budgeted under `## Mathlib gaps &
100	new material` as the β-core sub-piece, not as a separate target.
101	
102	### Route A (M3 off-critical-path) — Picard scheme via FGA
103	
104	Hilbert / Quot scheme representability + identity-component subgroup
105	scheme construction. Decomposition A.1–A.4 in `Jacobian.tex` §
106	"Route A — Picard scheme". Midpoint ~6070 LOC per
107	`analogies/m3-route-a-refresh-iter145.md` (the refresh enumerates per-
108	sub-piece LOC: A1 Hilbert/QCoh/Coh/flattening ~3775; A2 Quot post-A1
109	~1320; A3 identity-component ~975; sum ~6070). The autonomous loop
110	writes the missing Mathlib material in-tree.
111	
112	**Merit-based justification over Route B (Symⁿ + Stein).** Route B's
113	GIT-quotient construction needs Mathlib infrastructure for $S_n$-
114	quotients of schemes that is no closer to existence than Hilbert/Quot.
115	Route B also still requires an identity-component construction (Symⁿ
116	gives Picʳ for an explicit degree, not Pic⁰); the identity-component
117	+ Stein-factorization composite recovers the Jacobian. Net: Route B
118	is not materially cheaper than Route A in Mathlib-gap headcount, and
119	Route A's Hilbert/Quot infrastructure has cross-utility for downstream
120	algebraic-geometry consumers. Route B is documented in `Jacobian.tex`
121	as a historical alternative; the loop does not schedule work on it.
122	
123	### Alternative (still on the table) — over-$\bar k$ + Galois descent for M2.a
124	
125	Base-change to $\bar k$ where genus-0 forces $C_{\bar k} \cong \mathbb{P}^1_{\bar k}$,
126	apply rigidity for $\mathbb{P}^1_{\bar k}$ → group scheme (a much smaller
127	fact), descend to $k$ via fpqc descent of morphisms. Mathlib currently
128	lacks fpqc-descent-of-morphisms infrastructure, which would have to
129	be built up alongside this route; the chart-algebra envelope's
130	β-core Čech MV is structurally a one-time investment that pays for
131	itself across the M2.a + M2.b chain. **Rolling re-evaluation trigger**:
132	the chart-algebra cumulative envelope is **419 LOC landed + 380–800
133	LOC remaining = 799–1219**. The trigger fires conditionally: if the
134	remaining work consumes its upper bound (~800 LOC), the cumulative
135	reaches ~1219 and crosses the **1200-LOC trigger threshold**. The
136	planner pre-commits to re-opening this alternative via fresh
137	strategy-critic mid-iter dispatch when (a) the cumulative crosses
138	1200 LOC without closing, OR (b) a fresh prover lane returns
139	INCOMPLETE on a base-dependent sub-goal, OR (c) a new analogist
140	verdict surfaces an unanticipated Mathlib advance. A symmetric audit
141	of the alternatives is scheduled at the next strategic checkpoint
142	regardless.

223	**Caveat: false-converse pitfall.** The bare local-freeness-of-$\Omega$
224	implication is **mathematically false** — `Spec k → Spec k[t]` via
225	`t ↦ 0` is locally of finite presentation with $\Omega = 0$ but is
226	not flat, hence not smooth. The true converse needs
227	`Subsingleton (Algebra.H1Cotangent A B)`. The chapter
228	`Differentials.tex` discloses this; we do not state the false iff.

239	
240	**Soundness rules (operational, project-wide):**
241	
242	- **No new axioms.** Every closed declaration `lean_verify`s to
243	  kernel-only axioms (`propext, Classical.choice, Quot.sound`).
244	- **Sorry-must-be-named-declaration.** A `sorry` must be the body of
245	  a top-level named declaration. It must NOT be embedded inside a
246	  `letI` / `have` / anonymous-`fun` body inside a different
247	  declaration. Without this discipline, a buried `sorry` propagates
248	  a sorry-tainted value through `simp` consumers without surfacing a
249	  `declaration uses sorry` warning at the wrapping declaration.

32	## KaehlerDifferential.mem_range_algebraMap_of_D_eq_zero (L168 → L123 after edits)
33	
34	### Attempt 1
35	
36	- **Approach:** Signature inflation per planner (BR.1) — added `[CharZero k]`
37	  + `{n : ℕ}` + `[Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension n k B]`. Body
38	  builds (BR.2)–(BR.4) scaffolding via:
39	  - `Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.isStandardSmooth n` to
40	    extract `Algebra.IsStandardSmooth k B` (note: the relative-dimension
41	    class is a `Prop`, not registered as an instance, so dispatch through
42	    the named theorem).
43	  - `Algebra.IsStandardSmooth.free_kaehlerDifferential` to get
44	    `Module.Free B Ω[B⁄k]`.
45	  - `Module.Free.chooseBasis B Ω[B⁄k]` to pick a basis.
46	  - `(basis.coord i).compDer (KaehlerDifferential.D k B) : Derivation k B B`
47	    to extract the i-th coordinate derivation (BR.3).
48	  - `_hCoordVanish : ∀ i, ((basis.coord i).compDer D) b = 0` proved by
49	    `simp [Derivation.coe_comp, hDb]`.
50	- **Result:** PARTIAL — (BR.1)–(BR.4) closed in body; (BR.5) joint-kernel
51	  collapse remains structured sorry. `lean_diagnostic_messages` confirms
52	  clean compile.
53	- **Key insight:** `Derivation.coe_comp` unfolds `(f.compDer D) b = f (D b)`
54	  cleanly via `simp`; the (BR.3) extraction is a one-liner once basis is
55	  chosen.
56	- **Dead end / non-trivial finding:** `Differential.ContainConstants A B`
57	  is positioned for a SINGLE derivation `b' : B → B`, not for the
58	  INTERSECTION of multiple coordinate derivation kernels. The kernel of
59	  one `∂_i` alone is strictly larger than `range (algebraMap k B)` (e.g.
60	  `B = k[x_1, x_2]`, `∂_1 = ∂/∂x_1`, `ker ∂_1 = k[x_2] ⊋ k`). The (BR.5)
61	  joint-kernel collapse therefore requires a coordinate-induction
62	  argument on the standard-smooth presentation
63	  `B ≅ k[x_1, …, x_n, y_1, …, y_m] / (f_1, …, f_m)` with invertible
64	  Jacobian — substantive ~40–80 LOC Mathlib-PR-grade iter-150+ project work.
65	- **Next step:** Iter-150+ project lemma implementing
66	  `KaehlerDifferential.coordinateDeriv_jointKernel_collapse_of_charZero`
67	  (suggested name) that performs the coordinate induction.

81	
82	- **Approach:** Import `AlgebraicJacobian.Cotangent.ChartAlgebraS3` (Lane
83	  1's deliverable). Split the consolidated `⟨hPI, hSep⟩` sorry into
84	  `refine ⟨?_, ?_⟩` with two branches.
85	  - **hPI branch (b.2):** Documented the 5-step closure chain (base-
86	    change to `\bar k` ⇒ irreducible + reduced ⇒ integral ⇒ `Γ_{\bar k}`
87	    is a domain ⇒ via (S3.pi.1) `Γ ⊗ \bar k` is a domain ⇒
88	    unique-min-prime ⇒ via (S3.pi.2) purely inseparable). Body remains
89	    structured sorry; iter-150+ closure.
90	  - **hSep branch (b.1):** Wired up Lane 1's (S3.sep.1) via
91	    `haveI _hGR := AlgebraicGeometry.isGeometricallyReduced_Gamma_of_smooth`.
92	    Then constructed `FiniteDimensional k Γ` via:
93	    1. `Iso.commRingCatIsoToRingEquiv (Scheme.ΓSpecIso (CommRingCat.of k))`
94	       to extract a `RingEquiv` from the `Γ(Spec k, ⊤) ≅ k` iso.
95	    2. `RingEquiv.symm.finite` to get
96	       `((Scheme.ΓSpecIso (CommRingCat.of k)).inv.hom).Finite`.
97	    3. `_hAppTopFinite.comp hiso` (via `RingHom.Finite.comp`) gives
98	       `α.hom.Finite` where `α = (ΓSpecIso).inv ≫ (.appTop)`.
99	    4. `RingHom.finite_algebraMap.mp` converts `α.hom.Finite =
100	       (algebraMap k Γ).Finite` to `Module.Finite k Γ`.
101	    Then close via `Algebra.IsSeparable.of_isGeometricallyReduced_of_finite
102	    k _` (Lane 1's S3.sep.2 lemma).
103	- **Result:** RESOLVED for hSep (no project-internal sorry; routes purely
104	  through Lane 1's (S3.sep.1) + (S3.sep.2)). PARTIAL for hPI (structured
105	  sorry with full closure path documented).
106	- **Key insight:** The algebra-instance alignment was a worry — Lane 1's
107	  (S3.*) lemmas use `gammaAlgebra k X` (a `noncomputable def` wrapping
108	  `(gammaAlgebraMap k X).hom.toAlgebra`), while the consumer uses
109	  `letI algkΓ := α.hom.toAlgebra`. They're definitionally equal (since
110	  `α = gammaAlgebraMap k X`), and Lean's typeclass synthesis bridges
111	  them transparently. No explicit `change`/`convert` was needed.
112	- **Bridge construction:** The `FiniteDimensional k Γ` bridge from
113	  `_hAppTopFinite : (.appTop.hom).Finite` was the connecting piece that
114	  enabled hSep closure. Without it, (S3.sep.2)'s `[FiniteDimensional k F]`
115	  hypothesis couldn't be supplied.

117	  - `CategoryTheory.Iso.commRingCatIsoToRingEquiv` —
118	    `Mathlib.Algebra.Category.Ring.Basic`.
119	  - `RingEquiv.finite` — `Mathlib.RingTheory.Finiteness.Basic`.
120	  - `RingHom.Finite.comp` — `Mathlib.RingTheory.Finiteness.Basic`.
121	  - `RingHom.finite_algebraMap` — `Mathlib.RingTheory.Finiteness.Defs`.
122	  - `Algebra.IsSeparable.of_isGeometricallyReduced_of_finite` —
123	    Lane 1's `ChartAlgebraS3.lean` (body sorry, signature stable).
124	- **Next step (hPI):** Iter-150+ closure point. The chain requires
125	  base-changing `X` to `Spec \bar k` (via `pullback (X ↘ Spec k)
126	  (Spec.map (algebraMap k \bar k))`), propagating
127	  `GeometricallyIrreducible ⇒ IrreducibleSpace`, deriving `IsReduced` of
128	  the base-change (Mathlib gap: `Smooth ⇒ IsReduced` over a perfect
129	  base), combining to `IsIntegral X_{\bar k}`, getting `Γ_{\bar k}` is a
130	  domain, applying (S3.pi.1) iso to transport, and applying (S3.pi.2).
131	  Significant ~80–150 LOC.

11	errors).

17	non-negotiable deliverable; Lane 2 (`ChartAlgebra.lean` consumer) can
18	proceed against these signatures regardless of body status.
19	
20	## Helper definitions exported
21	
22	* `AlgebraicGeometry.gammaAlgebraMap k X` — the canonical `k → Γ(X, O_X)`
23	  ring map for `X` over `Spec k`.
24	* `AlgebraicGeometry.gammaAlgebra k X` — the corresponding
25	  `Algebra k Γ(X, O_X)` instance (`@[reducible] noncomputable def`).
26	  Definitionally equals the local `algkΓ` constructed inside
27	  `constants_integral_over_base_field` (L316 of `ChartAlgebra.lean`),
